title: Dungeon Crusade - Part Five - The Tomb of Kaladar
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v0kp6W42yf4

A short setup-and-state-of-play video. Daniel (@dungeondive) lays out the **Tomb of Kaladar** — the map he has been waiting for. It is unapologetically a death trap: trap icons in places you cannot route around, environmental hazard squares painted into the board (a bat sanctuary, spike pit, oscillating pendulums, a black rock viper pit, a noxious vapour chamber, and a chamber of blades). He admits he is house-ruling an extra healing potion or elixir to each hero to give them a fighting chance.

The opening minion mix is shown — orc archer, cultist of zuul, an *elite* skeletal grunt with the shield deflect ability, and a vanilla wraith. The first quest table pairs **Diary of a Madman** (collect four pages of Aleister Kaladar's diary from his study, the desecrated memorial east, and the prison chamber) with the side quest **Ashes to Ashes** (recover four villager bodies before Kaladar can raise them as a skeletal army).

Most of the back half is small-table craft. Daniel demos a custom initiative track sheet, a combat cheat sheet, a hero binder with health/essence/affliction tracking on its own page, and shrunken-down replacements for the official village and dungeon UI boards. He notes Roger has asked to take Daniel's homemade aids and re-skin them with proper Dungeon Crusade graphics. A poll on the official Facebook group puts a first full game at around eight hours.

----

Daniel quietly hands his heroes an extra potion before stepping into a board he knows will kill them. Where do you draw the line between adjusting difficulty and sanding off the experience the designer built?
